summary: Exodus 4:24 presents the enigmatic episode where YHWH encounters Moses (or his son) and 'seeks to kill him.' The ancient versions reveal sustained efforts to clarify the ambiguous pronominal antecedents, to soften the disturbing divine violence against God's own chosen messenger, and in Targumic traditions, to integrate halakhic concerns about circumcision. The Septuagint's subtle shifts and the Targums' explicit theological glosses demonstrate active interpretive intervention to render the passage less morally problematic and ritually intelligible.
summary_plain: Exodus 4:24 describes God trying to kill someone—either Moses or his son—in a shocking and confusing episode. Ancient translators struggled with this violent and unclear story, so they changed the wording to make it less disturbing, clarified who was being attacked, and added explanations about circumcision to make the passage fit their religious teachings.

revisions.mt_reading: And it happened on the way at the lodging place that YHWH met him and sought to kill him (וַיִּפְגְּשֵׁהוּ יְהוָה וַיְבַקֵּשׁ הֲמִיתוֹ)
revisions.revised_reading: Targum Onqelos: 'an angel of the LORD sought to kill him'; Targum Pseudo-Jonathan: 'an angel sent from before the LORD sought to kill him'
revisions.evidence: The Targumic replacement of YHWH as direct agent with 'an angel of the LORD' (מַלְאָכָא) constitutes classic intermediary insertion to distance God from potentially offensive direct action. The Hebrew allows no angelic mediator; the subject is unambiguously YHWH. This is not a translation issue but theological buffering, consistent with widespread Targumic anthropomorphism avoidance patterns (Grossfeld, The Targum Onqelos to Exodus). The use of 'sent from before the LORD' (Ps-J) further employs the Aramaic technical term 'מִן קֳדָם' to create metaphysical distance.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic translations changed the story so that an angel, not God himself, tried to kill Moses. This made the story less shocking by putting a messenger between God and the violent act.
scholarly_citations: Grossfeld, The Targum Onqelos to Exodus, pp. 19-20
revisions.counter_arguments: One could argue this reflects legitimate exegetical clarification of an implicit angelic agent already present in the Hebrew worldview, but the universal Targumic pattern of replacing direct divine violence with angelic mediation suggests systematic theological motivation rather than textual fidelity.

revisions.mt_reading: and sought to kill him (וַיְבַקֵּשׁ הֲמִיתוֹ) — pronominal antecedent ambiguous: Moses or the son?
revisions.revised_reading: LXX: ἐζήτει αὐτὸν ἀποκτεῖναι (sought to kill him) — but some manuscripts and traditions suggest clarifying interpretations favoring the son as object
revisions.evidence: While the LXX formally renders the Hebrew closely, textual and interpretive traditions within Hellenistic Judaism (reflected in early patristic citations) show efforts to clarify that the object of divine violence is the uncircumcised son, not Moses the prophet. Philo's silence on this passage and later Christian exegesis that emphasizes the son (Origen, Hom. Ex. 5.5) suggest discomfort with direct divine assault on the covenant mediator. The pronominal ambiguity is exploited to shift violence away from Moses.
revisions.evidence_plain: Greek-speaking Jews and early Christians read the passage to mean God was angry at Moses's uncircumcised son, not at Moses himself, making the violence seem more justified as punishment for breaking the circumcision law.
scholarly_citations: Wevers, Notes on the Greek Text of Exodus, pp. 53-54
revisions.counter_arguments: The LXX's literal rendering could suggest no theological motivation, merely faithful translation; however, the reception history and surrounding interpretive traditions indicate active disambiguation motivated by theological discomfort.

revisions.mt_reading: Narrative presents circumcision performed by Zipporah as emergency ritual response to divine threat, with cryptic phrase 'bridegroom of blood' (חֲתַן דָּמִים)
revisions.revised_reading: Targum Pseudo-Jonathan expands: 'Because of the blood of this circumcision the decree has been annulled... Now the angel said: Give thanks before the LORD who has forgiven the blood of the circumcision of this child.' Neofiti adds: 'How precious is the blood of circumcision which has saved this bridegroom from the hands of the angel of death'
revisions.evidence: The Targumic expansions explicitly theologize the circumcision as covenantal atonement that nullifies a divine decree (גְּזֵרָה), reflecting developed proto-rabbinic halakhic theology where circumcision blood has expiatory efficacy (cf. m. Nedarim 3:11; Mek. Pisha 5). The MT offers no such theological gloss; the Targums import a fully articulated ritual-theological framework absent from the Hebrew. The angel-of-death motif further distances direct divine violence while integrating folk-angelology.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Aramaic versions added long explanations saying that the circumcision blood itself saved Moses by canceling God's punishment, reflecting later Jewish teaching about circumcision as a ritual that brings forgiveness and protection.
scholarly_citations: Alexander, The Targum of Canticles, pp. 23-24 (comparative angelology)
Vermes, Scripture and Tradition in Judaism, pp. 178-179
revisions.counter_arguments: It could be argued these expansions merely make explicit what is implicit in the narrative logic of the Hebrew, but the specific vocabulary of 'decree,' 'annulled,' and 'angel of death' belong to later rabbinic technical discourse not present in the exodus text.

revisions.mt_reading: So Zipporah took a flint and cut off her son's foreskin and touched his feet (וַתִּגַּע לְרַגְלָיו)
revisions.revised_reading: Peshitta: 'and cast it before his feet' (ܘܫܕܬ ܩܕܡ ܪܓܠܘܗܝ) — changes 'touched' to 'cast/threw'
revisions.evidence: The Peshitta's substitution of 'cast before' for 'touched' (Hebrew נגע) avoids the cultic-sexual ambiguity of touching 'feet' (רַגְלַיִם, possible euphemism for genitals). This harmonizes with Levitical purity concerns about contact with blood and circumcision flesh, making Zipporah's action less physically intimate and more ritually proper. The Syriac verb ܫܕܐ suggests deliberate distancing rather than direct contact, softening the visceral, potentially improper physical touch by a woman.
revisions.evidence_plain: The Syriac translation changed the story from Zipporah touching Moses (or her son) with the bloody foreskin to her throwing it at his feet, avoiding awkward questions about a woman touching male genitals with blood.
scholarly_citations: Weitzman, The Syriac Version of the Old Testament, pp. 81-83
revisions.counter_arguments: The verb change could reflect a legitimate semantic range of the Hebrew נגע or variant Vorlage, though the consistent pattern of the Peshitta avoiding cultic-sexual ambiguity suggests theological motivation.

revisions.mt_reading: YHWH met him and sought to kill him (וַיִּפְגְּשֵׁהוּ יְהוָה וַיְבַקֵּשׁ הֲמִיתוֹ)
revisions.revised_reading: Vulgate: 'occurrit ei Dominus et voluit occidere eum' (the Lord met him and wished to kill him) — 'voluit' (wished/willed) slightly softens 'sought' (בקש)
revisions.evidence: Jerome's use of 'voluit' (to wish, will) rather than a more forceful verb like 'quaesivit' (to seek actively) or 'conatus est' (to attempt) subtly mitigates the aggressive divine action. While 'voluit' can mean 'sought,' its primary semantic field emphasizes divine volition and sovereignty rather than active pursuit, potentially framing the episode as testing or intentional pedagogy rather than raw hostility. This aligns with Jerome's broader tendency to preserve divine dignity in translation.
revisions.evidence_plain: Jerome's Latin translation used a word meaning 'willed' or 'wished' rather than 'sought,' making God's action sound more like a deliberate test than a violent attack.
scholarly_citations: Kamesar, Jerome, Greek Scholarship, and the Hebrew Bible, pp. 112-114
revisions.counter_arguments: Latin 'volo' regularly translates Hebrew בקש in the Vulgate, so this may simply be standard lexical equivalence without theological nuance; however, in contexts of divine violence, Jerome's lexical choices often show careful theological calibration.

dominant_strategy: Softening is the dominant revision strategy across all major traditions. The profound theological discomfort with YHWH directly and violently attacking his newly commissioned prophet Moses drives angelic intermediation (Targums), pronominal disambiguation to shift violence to the son (LXX reception), distancing of physical contact (Peshitta), and lexical mitigation (Vulgate). This offensive divine violence motivated more revision activity than any other feature of the passage.
overall_assessment: The textual history of Exodus 4:24-26 reveals a cross-traditional interpretive crisis provoked by the passage's depiction of inexplicable, near-fatal divine violence against God's own messenger. Tradents in Aramaic, Greek, Syriac, and Latin traditions deployed overlapping strategies—angelic mediation, expiatory circumcision theology, pronominal clarification, and lexical softening—to render the episode theologically coherent and morally acceptable. The Targumic tradition most radically transforms the passage through proto-rabbinic halakhic exegesis, converting raw divine assault into a pedagogical crisis resolved by covenantal blood ritual. This multi-traditional convergence on softening and theological buffering situates Exodus 4:24 among the most intensively revised 'scandal passages' in the Pentateuch, comparable to Genesis 6:1-4 and Exodus 32, where divine actions or characteristics challenged emerging monotheistic theological norms.
overall_plain: This short, strange story about God attacking Moses was so disturbing to ancient translators that nearly every tradition changed it in some way. They inserted angels to distance God from the violence, clarified that God was angry at the uncircumcised son rather than Moses, or added explanations about how circumcision stopped God's anger. These changes show how uncomfortable later readers were with the idea of God violently attacking the very person he just chose to lead Israel.

bibcrit_assessment.title: Universal Discomfort with Unmediated Divine Violence Against Prophets
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The convergence of independent textual traditions (Targumic, LXX reception, Peshitta, Vulgate) on strategies to mitigate or explain away YHWH's direct, hostile action against Moses reveals a cross-cultural theological boundary: God's covenant agents must not be arbitrary victims of divine violence. This pattern suggests the passage preserves archaic, pre-monotheistic theological material (divine caprice, apotropaic ritual) that later tradents found incompatible with developed covenant theology and prophetic commissioning narratives. The revisions mark a fault line between early Israelite religious phenomenology and Second Temple/rabbinic theological coherence.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: Every ancient tradition struggled with this story because it contradicts the basic idea that God protects, rather than attacks, the prophets he chooses. The changes reveal a shift from older, more unpredictable ideas about God to later, more consistent theological beliefs.
